# Unofficial Meeting Notes — Conroe ISD Board Workshop

**Date:** April 7, 2020
**Meeting Type:** Regular Workshop (Special Meeting)
**Format:** Virtual via Zoom Video Conferencing (per Governor Abbott's temporary suspension of Texas Open Meetings Act provisions)
**Called to Order:** 6:00 PM
**Adjourned:** 7:30 PM

> ⚠️ **These are unofficial notes** based on an auto-generated transcript of the meeting. They are not official minutes and may contain errors or omissions.

---

## Public Comment

No public comments were submitted via the district website forum. (Due to virtual format, comments were accepted only through the online public comment form.)

---

## Agenda Item 1: COVID-19 Response Update

**Presented by:** Dr. Chris Hines (with input from Dr. Curtis Null and Darrin Rice)

### District Priorities During COVID-19:
1. Keeping staff and students safe / reducing spread of COVID-19
2. Continuity of learning
3. Providing nutrition and other supports to students
4. Communicating with the learning community
5. Continuity of business operations

### Operational Timeline:
- **Week of March 16–20:** District closed; limited operations for planning
- **March 23:** Moved to limited operations (planning, technology distribution, material distribution); adjusted hours to minimize contact
- **March 30 onward:** "Essential operations" mode — very few people in buildings, primarily for food preparation and essential functions

### Key Operational Updates:

#### Health Services
- Health information phone line staffed by nurses (8 AM–3 PM)
- Daily updates with Texas Dept. of State Health Services and CDC
- Daily webinars with district nurses
- Screening protocols established (temperature checks, questionnaires for workers)
- Distributed protective equipment inventory
- Shared resources (temporal thermometers) with local fire and EMS departments
- Collaborated with Montgomery County Office of Emergency Management

#### Meal Distribution
- **10 distribution sites:** Austin Elementary, Caney Creek, Conroe High School, Knox Junior High, Grand Oaks, Hawk, Oak Ridge High School, College Park, The Woodlands, Washington Junior High
- Two meals distributed on Tuesdays; three meals on Thursdays
- As of the prior Thursday: **205,868 total meals distributed**
- On April 7 alone: **over 32,000 meals** distributed
- Meal distribution volumes have been gradually increasing

**Board Discussion on Meal Funding (Scott Kidd):**
- Dr. Kidd asked about potential donations for meal programs
- Dr. Hines and Dr. Null clarified that distributed meals are **reimbursable** — no debt is accumulating for families
- For community members wanting to donate, the district recommended the **Montgomery County Food Bank** as the most impactful donation destination
- Food bank supports the Buddy Backpack program (weekend meals), Meals on Wheels for homeless students, and mobile pantries
- **12 Communities in Schools sites** have sponsored mobile food pantries
- Matthew's Birthday Wish continues delivering to 5 campuses

#### Staff & Family Support
- Community resource lists shared with counseling staff and families
- Employee Assistance Program (EAP) reminders sent to staff
- Counseling staff reaching out regularly to check on families
- Spanish hotline operated by community outreach department
- Coping resources and stress management guidance distributed

#### Continuity of Learning (Curriculum & Instruction)
- Curriculum narrowed to **1–2 essential standards** for the final nine weeks
- Webpages developed for teacher and parent resources (live since March 20); updated weekly in English and Spanish
- Support provided for bilingual/ESL, GT, special education, 504, and dyslexia students
- Canvas (LMS) at **all-time high usage**
- Resources contacted by districts from multiple states, including Hawaii

**Fourth Nine Weeks Grading Policy Changes:**
- Focus on key objectives necessary for next grade level success
- Limited to **one graded assignment per week** (formative only)
- No penalties for late work
- **Grade for 4th nine weeks cannot be lower than 3rd nine weeks grade** (can only go up)
- No major grades or final exams
- Remote access limitations will not be a barrier — campuses delivering technology or paper packets

#### Technology Support
- **Over 4,000 Chromebooks** distributed to students
- **300 Chromebooks with built-in internet** distributed
- **500+ hotspots** deployed (in addition to previously existing ones)
- Internet bandwidth increased from **6 gigs to 10 gigs**
- Added internet redundancy for outage protection
- Increased monitoring of network systems and servers
- Cybersecurity awareness training for employees working from home
- Single sign-on authentication capacity expanded
- **138 students enrolled** remotely during closure
- Office 365 available to all staff and students at home (district-wide license)
- Office phones configured for remote forwarding

#### Payroll (under Becky Davis)
- Processed April 1 pay period; working on April 15
- Processing manual checks for returned direct deposits
- Stopped student loan deductions where applicable
- Notifying staff of available relief programs
- Handling W-2 requests, TRS reporting forms, absence/leave processing
- Responding to numerous employee inquiries about pay

#### Other Departments
- **Finance:** Staffing projections, closing out cancelled travel/events, working on next year's budget
- **Accounts Payable:** Continuing to pay bills
- **Purchasing:** Assisting with hotspot/technology purchases, food delivery logistics
- **Planning/Construction:** Monitoring ongoing and new projects
- **HR:** Continuing recruitment, managing postings, resignations, retirements; ensuring part-time staff and long-term subs are paid; dealing with cancelled certification test complications; working on allocations and benefit information
- **Custodial/Maintenance:** Opening sites for food prep, E-misting/cleaning feeding sites and offices, checking facilities 3 times/week, moving portables for upcoming construction, maintaining mechanical systems/water wells/wastewater plants, limited grounds maintenance

#### Communications
- District status updates via SchoolMessenger
- Several **Facebook Live events** with Dr. Null (next one: April 13 at 6 PM)
- Videos, book readings, life lessons series released
- **Virtual choir video:** 30,000 views in 12 hours
- Supporting rescheduling of events (job fair, car show, foundation breakfast, early childhood fair, groundbreaking)
- Standardized campus email addresses created
- All communications translated to Spanish
- Facilitating Zoom conferencing for district operations
- Processing mail and postage
- Monitoring voicemail/email at all sites during business hours (8–3)

#### Transportation Funding Discussion
- **Board member question** about transportation savings
- Dr. Null clarified: District is **not receiving transportation funding** during closure (per-mileage reimbursement)
- Estimated **$3–4 million less** in transportation funding for the last quarter
- Bus drivers still being paid standard hours — **no net savings realized**

---

## Agenda Item 2: Oak Ridge High School Master Plan Update

**Presented by:** Mr. Easy Foster (with Dr. Null)

### Background:
- Oak Ridge HS is the district's **second oldest high school**; will become the oldest upon completion of Conroe HS renovations
- Phase 1 of the November bond package was primarily **mechanical updates**
- City of Shenandoah approached the district about a broader site reimagining

### Proposed Concept:
- **Reroute Oak Ridge School Road** around the campus instead of through it
- Current road bisects the campus between Oak Ridge 9th grade and the main campus, creating safety and traffic issues
- Potential to **connect the 9th grade campus to the main campus** (either via building across the road or a secured walkway)
- Potential new intersection at **Johnson Road** that would create a direct connection from Oak Ridge HS through Hammond to Woodforest Stadium
- Initial conversations with City of Shenandoah, county traffic engineer, and Precinct 4 Chief of Staff (physical meetings delayed due to COVID-19)
- Early indications suggest the county and surrounding community would support the transition

### Budget Impact:
- Would use some current Phase 1 budget for the road project
- Some Phase 1 items would shift to Phase 2
- Would **not exceed overall budget** — just a reallocation

### Board Discussion:
- **John Moore** asked about acquiring full access rights to Oak Ridge School Road (similar to Wilson Road in Conroe). Foster noted conversations are ongoing; right-of-way is county-controlled; no reason to believe the county wouldn't allow the district to take it back.
- **Ray Sanders** asked about property ownership in the northeast corner of the site. Foster confirmed that corner is **not currently district property** but stated purchase is not necessarily required — multiple options exist.
- **Board member question** about athletic fields (baseball, softball, track, football) — Foster said it's too early to determine but many options would be explored.

### Outcome:
- **No vote requested** — workshop item for feedback only
- Board expressed no objections to continued investigation
- District will bring project information back for a formal vote in the future

---

## Agenda Item 3: 2020–2021 Preliminary Budget Overview

**Presented by:** Darrin Rice, Chief Financial Officer (with Dr. Null)

### Budget Objectives for 2020–2021:
- Opening **Stockton Junior High School** (new)
- Opening **12th grade at Grand Oaks High School**
- Accommodating estimated **1,500 new students** (total enrollment: ~66,298)
- Personnel allocation adjustments at elementary and intermediate levels
- General pay increase (cost-of-living)
- Addressing health insurance premium increases
- Funding remaining House Bill 3 requirements (full-day pre-K, Reading Academies K–3, dyslexia, CCMR, special ed, CTE programs)

### Fund Balance:
- Ended 2019 with **$140 million** fund balance
- Transferred $32 million (2019–2020): $12M to technology (2019 bond), $20M to capital maintenance fund
- Unassigned fund balance (8/31/19): **$134.7 million** (24.5% of budget)
- $24.8M over low-end target; $2.7M under high-end target
- Anticipating fund balance to **grow by ~$10 million** this year from prior year savings

### Tax Rate:
- CISD is **second lowest tax rate** in the Greater Houston area
- Current rate: **$1.23** — 15 cents below peer district average
- 4 cents below the closest peer district (Forney ISD)

### Tax Rate Compression (House Bill 3):
- Maximum Compressed Rate (MCR) calculated at **90.35 cents** (assuming 5.5% property value growth)
- With access to **one additional (5th) golden penny** allowed by the legislature:
  - **Proposed M&O rate: 95.35 cents** (decrease of 1.65 cents)
  - **Proposed I&S rate: 26 cents** (unchanged)
  - **Proposed total tax rate: $1.2135** (decrease of 1.656 cents)

### Revenue Projections:
| Source | Amount |
|--------|--------|
| Tax revenue increase (5.5% levy growth) | +$12.11M |
| State revenue increase (1,500 students + golden penny) | +$10.8M |
| Investment income decrease (Fed rate cuts) | -$1.7M |
| TRS in-kind (accounting entry) | +$3.5M |
| **Total estimated available funding** | **+$24.71M** |

### Proposed Salary Increases (Cost-of-Living):
| Employee Group | Proposed Increase | Cost |
|----------------|-------------------|------|
| Teachers, librarians, nurses, counselors | 3% | $7.7M |
| Administrative (education & business) | 2.5% | $1.9M |
| Paraprofessionals (admin & instructional) | 3.5% | $950K |
| Auxiliary (custodians, maintenance, etc.) | 3.5% | — |
| Bus drivers | Flat $1/hour (~5% at midpoint) | — |
| Auxiliary + transportation total | — | $1.5M |
| Police | 5% + certification pay | — |

**Total cost-of-living increases: ~$12.5 million**

**Note:** The teacher hiring schedule (3% / $1,800 raise for returning teachers, new starting salary of **$57,000**) was previously approved at a board meeting two weeks prior. Dr. Null noted the board could still adjust this if needed since no one has been hired under the new schedule yet.

#### Police Pay Discussion:
- Chief and Captain Blakelock conducted a regional salary study
- District was losing officers to other agencies due to base pay and lack of certification pay
- Current vacancies in the police department are difficult to fill
- Certification pay for master officers and years of service is a key retention tool

### Personnel Allocations:
- **225 new campus-level positions:** 125 teachers, 5 administrators, 16.5 professionals, 77.5 paraprofessionals — Cost: **$11.3M**
- **72 new support positions** (primarily custodians, maintenance, transportation) — Cost: **$3.5M**
- **Total: ~297 new positions at $14.85 million**

### Expenditure Summary:
| Category | Amount |
|----------|--------|
| Additional personnel for growth | $14.86M |
| Cost-of-living increases | $12.5M |
| Substitute pay increase | $300K |
| Employee retention stipend (removed) | (savings) |
| Pre-K transfer & buses | $9.2M |
| TRS in-kind | $3.5M |
| Other expenses (fuel, utilities, insurance, supplies) | $1.95M |
| **Total expenditure increase** | **$23.69M** |

### Projected Budget Summary:
| Item | Amount |
|------|--------|
| Beginning revenue | $555.62M |
| Total revenue increase | +$24.71M |
| Estimated total revenue | **$580.33M** |
| Estimated total expenditures | **$576.02M** |
| **Potential available funds (surplus)** | **$4.31M** |

### Property Values:
- Estimated growth: **5.5%** (adds ~$2 billion, total: ~$40.2 billion)
- Appraisal district confirms values trending around 5.5%
- Under HB 3, property value growth above 2.5% flows to the state on M&O side
- Lower interest rates are offsetting concerns about potential future property value slowdowns on the I&S (bond) side

### Board Discussion on Budget:

**Scott Kidd:** Asked about the golden penny — Dr. Null confirmed it generates approximately **$8 million** in revenue (state matches the penny). It will fund full-day pre-K (~$5.5M) and other HB 3 mandated programs. Vote will occur as part of **August budget adoption**.

**John Hubert** raised two concerns:
1. **Enrollment risk:** If enrollment were flat (zero growth), ~$10M in revenue would be lost, creating a ~$6M deficit. Dr. Null confirmed the $4.31M surplus plus natural attrition savings would provide a cushion.
2. **Property value risk:** Concern about COVID-19 impact on future appraisal values. Dr. Null noted even in recessions, values have grown at least ~3.5% due to continued homebuilding. On M&O side, growth above 2.5% goes to the state regardless, so impact is minimal. On bond side, historically low interest rates provide protection.

**President Williams:** Asked about voting requirements for the golden penny — Dr. Null clarified a **unanimous (100%) vote** is required.

### Health Insurance Update:

**Current Plan Performance:**
- Total revenues: $28.6M; Total expenses: $29.1M — **~$500K deficit**
- Five consecutive months in the red
- Large claims months (June–August) still ahead

**Market Trends:**
- Medical inflation: 5–7%
- CISD trending **8–10%** (above market) due to high-dollar claims penetrating stop-loss and high utilization
- District had outperformed market for past 4 years (3–4% range)
- Last year: employees received premium decreases via Kelsey program with increased benefits

**Outlook:**
- Plan design changes (tightening benefits) will be necessary
- Working with United Healthcare on deductible adjustments and premium increases
- **Plan design to be brought to April 21 board meeting**
- Employee Benefits Committee meeting: **April 15** to review plan design changes and premiums

**Ray Sanders** requested the district examine pharmacy/drug costs for potential savings (generic formulary, lower copays). Rice confirmed pharmacy is actually performing well but they will continue looking for savings opportunities across all areas.

---

## Key Takeaways

- **No votes were taken** — this was a workshop/information session only
- District is in **strong financial position** with robust fund balance, conservative budgeting practices, and contingency cushions
- **COVID-19 response** is extensive across all departments; over 205,000 meals distributed; 4,000+ Chromebooks deployed
- **Oak Ridge HS master plan** concept to reroute school road received positive initial feedback; investigation will continue
- **Proposed 2020–2021 budget** includes cost-of-living raises for all employee groups, 297 new positions, a **tax rate decrease of ~1.66 cents**, and a projected **$4.31M surplus**
- **Health insurance premiums will increase** — plan design changes coming to April 21 meeting
- Board expressed support for conservative approach to budgeting given economic uncertainty from COVID-19 and oil price declines
- Next board meeting (April 21) will be the first virtual meeting requiring formal votes — procedures for roll call/visible voting still being finalized
